pages.title: EXPOSГ‰ XIX REDUCTIVE GROUPS вЂ” GENERALITIES

blocks.html:
EXPOSГ‰ XIX
REDUCTIVE GROUPS вЂ” GENERALITIESby M. Demazure

The remainder of this Seminar is devoted to the study of reductive groups. Its main goal is the generalization of the classical results of Chevalley (BIBLE and TOHOKU) to arbitrary base schemes, the two central results being the uniqueness theorem (Exp. XXIII) and the existence theorem (Exp. XXV) for the вЂњpinnedвЂќ (В« Г©pinglГ©s В») reductive group schemes corresponding to prescribed вЂњroot dataвЂќ (В« donnГ©es radicielles В»). The proofs employed are inspired by those of Chevalley, the technique of schemes making it possible to give them increased efficiency.

The results of the first volume of the BIBLE (Exp. 1 to 13) will be used systematically. On the other hand, we shall prove directly over an arbitrary prescheme the results of the second volume (the isomorphism theorem in particular); knowledge of the proofs over an algebraically closed field is therefore not absolutely indispensable.

In the proof of these two fundamental results, we shall use only the most elementary results of the theory of groups of multiplicative type, contained for the most part in Exp. VIII and IX; on the other hand we shall make essential use of the results of ExposГ© XVIII. The reader especially interested in the existence and uniqueness theorems may, on a first reading, skip ExposГ©s X to XVII.

pages.title: 1. Reminders on groups over an algebraically closed field

blocks.html: 1. Reminders on groups over an algebraically closed field

In this section, \(k\) will always denote an algebraically closed field. As announced above, the only results of BIBLE used in the sequel are to be found in volume 1 (ExposГ©s I to XIII). All the results of BIBLE concerning semisimple groups are valid more generally for reductive groups (definition below), and their proof is identical, with the harmless modifications listed below:

• вЂ“ 9-06, definition 3 (!), see below.
  • вЂ“ 12-09, theorem 2, e) delete вЂњfiniteвЂќ.
  • вЂ“ 13-08, theorem 2, replace вЂњrankвЂќ by вЂњsemisimple rankвЂќ.
  • вЂ“ 13-12, corollary 2 to theorem 3, replace вЂњrankвЂќ by вЂњreductive rankвЂќ.

Let G be a smooth affine and connected \(k\)-group. The radical of G (BIBLE, 9-06, prop. 2) is the reduced subgroup associated with the identity component of the intersection of the Borel subgroups of G; it is also the largest solvable smooth connected normal subgroup of G; we shall denote it by \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\).
The unipotent radical of G is the unipotent part of the radical of G; it is also the largest unipotent smooth connected normal subgroup of G; we shall denote it by \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\).

If G is a smooth affine and connected \(k\)-group and if Q is a torus of G, then the centralizer \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) of Q in G is a closed subgroup of G, smooth (Exp. XI, 2.4) and connected (BIBLE, 6-14, th. 6 a)).

We have the fundamental relation: \[ \mathrm{rad}^u(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}) \cap \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q}). \] Indeed, by BIBLE, 12-09, cor. to th. 1, we have the relation \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q}))(k) \subset \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})(k)\). To prove the preceding equality, it therefore suffices to see that \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}) \cap \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is smooth and connected. If we let Q act on \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) by inner automorphisms, the preceding group is none other than the scheme of invariants of this action. Now:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.4

Let S be a prescheme, Q an S-torus, H an S-group prescheme on which Q acts. Suppose H is separated over S, so that the functor of invariants \(\mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{Q}}\) is representable (Exp. VIII, 6.5 d)). If H is smooth over S (resp. smooth, affine, and with connected fibers over S), then so is \(\mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{Q}}\).

Indeed, consider the semidirect product \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{H} \cdot \mathrm{Q}\). It is smooth over S (resp. smooth, affine and with connected fibers over S). We obviously have \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q}) = \mathrm{H}^{\mathrm{Q}}\), and the latter is smooth over S (Exp. XI, 2.4) (resp. smooth and affine over S, and with connected fibers (1.3)). Since Q is smooth, affine and with connected fibers over S, we are done.

Let us point out a special case of the preceding result: if G is a smooth affine and connected \(k\)-group and if T is a maximal torus of G, \[ \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{T} \cdot (\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) \cap \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})). \] Indeed (BIBLE, 6-14, th. 6 c)), \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) is a solvable group, hence the semidirect product of its maximal torus T and its unipotent radical.

By virtue of the density theorem (BIBLE, 6-13, th. 5), it follows that if G is a smooth affine and connected \(k\)-group, the union of the \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\), as T runs through the set of maximal tori of G, is dense in G.

Recall that the reductive rank of the smooth affine \(k\)-group G is the common dimension of the maximal tori of G. We shall denote it by \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/k)\) or \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G})\). For \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/k) = 0\), it is necessary and sufficient that G be unipotent (i.e. that \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\)), by BIBLE, 6-10, cor. 1 to th. 4.

If H is a normal subgroup of the smooth affine \(k\)-group G, the quotient G/H is affine and smooth (Exp. VI). Moreover (BIBLE, 7-05, th. 3, a) and c)), we have \[ \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{H}) + \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{H}). \]

We say that the smooth, affine and connected \(k\)-group G is reductive if \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\) is a torus, that is, if \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}) = e\). If G is a reductive \(k\)-group and if Q is a torus of G, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is reductive (1.3). In particular, if T is a maximal torus of G, \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{T}\). It follows that the center of G is contained in every maximal torus, and hence is diagonalizable. The radical of G is then the (unique) maximal torus of \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\): indeed, the latter is a smooth normal solvable connected subgroup, hence contained in \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\); conversely \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\) is a torus which is normal in G, hence central (BIBLE, 4-07, cor. to prop. 2).

If G is reductive and if \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) \neq \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G})\), then \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) \geqslant 2\). Indeed, this difference is always even (cf. 1.10 below).

Let G be a smooth affine and connected \(k\)-group and H a smooth connected normal subgroup. Then \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{H}) = (\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G}) \cap \mathrm{H})^0_{\mathrm{rГ©d}}\) and \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{H}) = (\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}) \cap \mathrm{H})^0_{\mathrm{rГ©d}}\), as one sees at once. In particular, if G is reductive, so is H.
Let \(f : \mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}'\) be a surjective morphism of smooth affine and connected \(k\)-groups. Then \(f(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}')\).

It is first of all clear that \(f\) sends \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) into \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}')\). Introducing \(\mathrm{H} = (f^{-1}(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}')))^0_{\mathrm{rГ©d}} \supset \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\), we have \(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{H}) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) and we are reduced to the case where \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{H}\), i.e. where \(\mathrm{G}'\) is unipotent. Since the union of the \(\mathrm{T} \cdot \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) (T running through the set of maximal tori of G) is dense in G, the union of the \(f(\mathrm{T})f(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}))\) is dense in \(\mathrm{G}'\); but \(f(\mathrm{T})\) consists of semisimple elements, hence \(f(\mathrm{T}) = e\), \(\mathrm{G}'\) being unipotent; this shows that \(f(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G}))\) is dense in \(\mathrm{G}'\), hence \(f(\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})) = \mathrm{G}'\) (cf. BIBLE, 5-13, lemma 4). In particular, every quotient of a reductive group is reductive.
(Nota, one can prove under the same hypotheses that \(f(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})) = \mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G}')\).)

We say that the smooth affine and connected \(k\)-group G is semisimple if \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G}) = e\), that is, if G is reductive and \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\) is finite. If G is any smooth affine connected \(k\)-group, then \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\) is semisimple (BIBLE, 9-06, prop. 2), and \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{G})\) is reductive. We call the semisimple rank of G, and denote by \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}/k)\) or \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G})\), the reductive rank of \(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\).

If G is reductive, we thus have \[ \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}) + \dim(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})). \] If G is a smooth affine and connected group and if Q is a central subtorus of G, then G/Q is semisimple if and only if G is reductive and \(\mathrm{Q} = \mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\). Indeed, if G/Q is semisimple, then \(\mathrm{Q} \supset \mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\), so \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\) is a torus, so G is reductive, so \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\) is the maximal torus of \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\), so \(\mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{Q}\). If G is reductive and if Q is a central subgroup, then (G/Q is reductive and) \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q})\).

If K is an algebraically closed extension of \(k\) and if G is a smooth affine connected \(k\)-group, then G is reductive (resp. semisimple) if and only if \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{K}}\) is, and we have \[ \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/k) = \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{K}}/\mathrm{K}), \] \[ \mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}/k) = \mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{K}}/\mathrm{K}). \]

Let G be a smooth and connected \(k\)-group and let T be a torus of G. The Lie algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) of G decomposes under the action of T (via \(\mathrm{T} \to \mathrm{G}\) and the adjoint action of G) as \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{g}^0 \oplus \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha, \] where the \(\alpha \in \mathrm{R}\) are nontrivial characters of T and where the \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) are \(\neq 0\). The characters \(\alpha \in \mathrm{R}\) are called the roots of G with respect to T. By Exp. II, 5.2.3, (i), we have \[ \mathfrak{g}^0 = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})). \] In particular, T is its own centralizer if and only if \(\mathfrak{g}^0 = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{T})\), (Exp. XII, 6.6 b), or 1.3 in the case where G is affine). This condition implies that T is maximal and that \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G}) \subset \mathrm{T}\), hence (Exp. XII 8.8 d)) \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G}) = \bigcap_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\); moreover \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\) is then affine, hence \(\mathrm{G} \to \mathrm{G}/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}(\mathrm{G})\) is affine; as this last group is affine (Exp. XII 6.1), so is G.

When G is reductive and T maximal, the roots in the preceding sense coincide with the roots in the sense of BIBLE, 12-04, def. 1; the latter are indeed roots in the sense of this section (BIBLE, 13-05, th. 1, c)) and there are \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T})\) of them (BIBLE, 13-11, cor. 2 to th. 3). Moreover, if \(\alpha\) is a root, then so is \(-\alpha\) (BIBLE, 12-05, cor. to prop. 1). (As usual, we write the group structure of \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,k})\) additively or multiplicatively, as convenient.) It follows that \[ \dim(\mathrm{G}) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{Card}(\mathrm{R}) \] is always even.

The semisimple rank of the reductive group G is the rank of the subset R of the \(\mathbb{Q}\)-vector space \(\mathrm{Hom}_{k\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,k}) \otimes \mathbb{Q}\) (BIBLE, 13-08, th. 2).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.11

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, G a smooth affine connected \(k\)-group, T a torus of G, and \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\) the Weyl group of G with respect to T. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) G is reductive, T is maximal, \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}) = 1\).

(ii) G is reductive, T is maximal, \(\mathrm{G} \neq \mathrm{T}\); there exists a subtorus Q of T, of codimension 1 in T, central in G.

(iii) G is not solvable and \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}) \leqslant 2\).

(iv) \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{T}) \neq e\) and \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}) \leqslant 2\).

(v) \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{T}) = (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_k\) and \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}) = 2\).

Moreover, under these conditions, there are exactly two roots of G with respect to T; they are opposite. Under the conditions of (ii), \(\mathrm{Q} = \mathrm{rad}(\mathrm{G})\).

Obviously (v) \(\Rightarrow\) (iv). We have (iv) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii) by BIBLE, 6-04, cor. 3 to th. 1. Let us prove (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii): let U be the unipotent radical of G; we know that G/U is reductive and is not a torus (otherwise G would be solvable); we therefore have (1.6) \[ \dim(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}) \geqslant 2\ ; \] but \[ \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}) + \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{U}) = \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}), \] whence \[ \dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{U}) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) = \dim(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{U}) \geqslant 2 \geqslant \dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}) \geqslant \dim(\mathrm{G}) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}). \] This gives \(\dim(\mathrm{U}) = 0\), so G is reductive, \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) = \dim(\mathrm{T})\), so T is maximal, and \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}) = 2\). By BIBLE, 10-09, prop. 8, there exists a singular torus Q of codimension 1 in T; then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is reductive (1.6), not solvable (definition of a singular torus), hence \(\dim(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})) - \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}) \geqslant 2\), which proves that \(\mathrm{G} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) and that Q is central in G.

Let us prove (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i). We know that G/Q is reductive (1.7) and that \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}) = 1\), so \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}) = 0\) or 1. The first case is impossible, for it would imply \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}) = 0\), hence \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{T}\); we therefore have \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}) = \mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{Q}) = 1\), which proves that G/Q is semisimple; hence Q is the radical of G and \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}) = 1\).

Finally let us prove (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (v). If Q is the radical of G, we have \(\dim(\mathrm{T}) - \dim(\mathrm{Q}) = 1\) and Q is central in G, so \(\mathrm{G} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\), which proves that Q is a singular torus; by BIBLE, 11-07, th. 2, we have \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_k\); by BIBLE, 12-01, lemma 1, we have \(\dim(\mathrm{G}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}) = 2\). There are therefore at most two roots of G with respect to T, but there are at least two of them, opposite to each other (1.10).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.12

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, G a smooth and connected \(k\)-group, T a torus of G, R the set of roots of G with respect to T, and \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{g}^0 \oplus \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha, \qquad \text{with } \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \neq 0, \] the decomposition of \(\mathfrak{g}\) under \(\mathrm{Ad}(\mathrm{T})\). For each \(\alpha \in \mathrm{R}\), let \(\mathrm{T}_\alpha\) be the maximal torus of \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}_\alpha)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) G is affine, reductive; T is maximal.

(ii) \(\mathfrak{g}^0 = \mathfrak{t}\), each \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) \((\alpha \in \mathrm{R})\) is reductive.

(iii) \(\mathfrak{g}^0 = \mathfrak{t}\), each \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) \((\alpha \in \mathrm{R})\) is of dimension 1; and if \(\alpha, \beta \in \mathrm{R}\) and \(k \in \mathbb{Q}\) are such that \(\beta = k\alpha\), then \(k = \pm 1\); for each \(\alpha \in \mathrm{R}\), there exists a \(w_\alpha \in \mathrm{G}(k)\) which normalizes T, centralizes \(\mathrm{T}_\alpha\), but does not centralize T.

Moreover, under these conditions, each \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) is of semisimple rank 1 and we have \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha) = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\).

If G is affine and reductive and if T is maximal, each \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) is affine and reductive (1.6), with maximal torus T; moreover, T is its own centralizer (1.6), hence \(\mathfrak{g}^0 = \mathfrak{t}\), which proves (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (ii).

On the other hand, \(\mathfrak{g}^0 = \mathfrak{t}\) implies that T is maximal and G affine (cf. 1.10), hence each \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) is affine (1.3). In any case, by Exp. II, 5.2.2 we have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha) = \mathfrak{g}^{\mathrm{T}_\alpha} = \mathfrak{g}^0 \oplus \coprod_{\beta \in \mathrm{R} \cap \mathbb{Q}\alpha} \mathfrak{g}^\beta. \] We therefore have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha) \supset \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}, \] which implies \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha \neq \mathrm{T}\); by 1.11, this at once gives (ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (iii) and the complements.

It remains to prove (ii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i); we already know that (ii) implies that G is affine. Let U be its unipotent radical; it is normal in G, and its Lie algebra is invariant under \(\mathrm{Ad}(\mathrm{T})\). We therefore have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \mathfrak{g}^0 + \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \mathfrak{g}^\alpha. \] By 1.3, we have \[ \mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{T} = \mathrm{U} \cap \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{T}) = e, \] \[ \mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{Z}_\alpha = \mathrm{U} \cap \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}_\alpha) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}_\alpha)) = \mathrm{rad}^u(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha) = e. \] We therefore have \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \mathfrak{g}^0 = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \mathfrak{t} = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{T}) = 0, \] \[ \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \subset \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) \cap \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{Z}_\alpha) = 0\ ; \] hence \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{U}) = 0\) and \(\mathrm{U} = \{e\}\).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 1.13

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, G a smooth and connected \(k\)-group, T a torus of G, R the set of roots of G with respect to T, and \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{g}^0 \oplus \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \] as above. For each \(\alpha \in \mathrm{R}\), let \(\mathrm{T}_\alpha\) be the maximal torus of \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) and \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}_\alpha)\). The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) G is affine, semisimple; T is maximal.

(ii) \(\mathfrak{g}^0 = \mathfrak{t}\), each \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) is reductive and \(\bigcap_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) is finite.

pages.title: 2. Reductive group schemes. Definitions and first properties

blocks.html: 2. Reductive group schemes. Definitions and first properties
If G is a group prescheme over S, the following properties are equivalent:
(i) G is affine and smooth over S, with connected fibers.
(ii) G is affine and flat over S, of finite presentation over S, with integral geometric fibers.
These properties are stable under base change and local for (fpqc).

If the S-group prescheme G satisfies the preceding conditions, and if Q is a torus (Exp. IX, 1.3) of G, then \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is representable by a closed subgroup prescheme of G which also satisfies these conditions (1.3 and Exp. XI, 6.11).

We shall systematically use the following notation: if S is a prescheme and \(s\) a point of S, we shall denote by \(\overline{s}\) the spectrum of an algebraic closure \(\overline{k(s)}\) of \(k(s)\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and affine over S, with connected fibers, and T a torus of G. The set of \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) is a reductive \(\overline{s}\)-group, of semisimple rank 1 and with maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\), is an open subset U of S.

Since G and T are flat over S, the function \[ s \longmapsto \dim(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}/\overline{s}) - \dim(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}/\overline{s}) \] is locally constant; let \(\mathrm{U}_1\) be the open set of points of S where it equals 2. Now consider the Weyl group (6.3) \[ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}). \] The function \[ s \longmapsto \text{number of points of } \mathrm{W}(\mathrm{T})_{\overline{s}} \] is lower semicontinuous (6.3 (ii)). Let \(\mathrm{U}_2\) be the set of points of S where this function is \(> 1\); it is an open set. It follows from 1.11 that the set of \(s\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) is reductive, of semisimple rank 1, with maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\), is \(\mathrm{U} = \mathrm{U}_1 \cap \mathrm{U}_2\).

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.4

The group \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{T})_{\mathrm{U}}\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{U}}\). Indeed, it is Г©tale and finite over U (6.3 (iii)); since the automorphism functor of \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{U}}\) is the unit group, it suffices to verify the assertion locally; but it is immediate if the Algebra which defines \(\mathrm{W}(\mathrm{T})_{\mathrm{U}}\) is a free \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{U}}\)-Module.

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 2.5

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme affine and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibers, and \(s_0\) a point of S. Suppose G is flat over S at \(e_{\mathrm{G}}(s_0)\) and that the geometric fiber \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}_0}\) is (reduced and) reductive (resp. semisimple). There exist an open subset U of S containing \(s_0\) and a surjective Г©tale morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{U}\) such that:

(i) \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{U}}\) is smooth over S, with reductive (resp. semisimple) geometric fibers, of constant reductive rank and constant semisimple rank.

(ii) \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) possesses a maximal torus T, the Weyl group (cf. 6.3) \[ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T})/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{T} \] being finite over \(\mathrm{S}'\).

Since G is flat over S at \(e_{\mathrm{G}}(s_0)\) and \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}_0}\) is reduced, G is smooth over S at \(e_{\mathrm{G}}(s_0)\). Since G has connected fibers, we may, after shrinking S, suppose G smooth over S (VIB 3.10). By Exp. XI, 5.8 a) and Exp. X, 4.5 (see also 6.1), there exist an Г©tale \(\mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}\), a point \(s''_0\) above \(s_0\) and a trivial torus T of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}''}\), maximal at \(s''_0\). Since an Г©tale morphism is open and the assertions of (i) are local for (Г©t.), we may therefore suppose that G possesses a trivial torus T, maximal at \(s_0\). So write \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbf{D}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{M})\) and let \[ \mathfrak{g} = \coprod_{m \in \mathrm{M}} \mathfrak{g}^m, \] be the decomposition of \(\mathfrak{g} = \mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) under \(\mathrm{Ad}(\mathrm{T})\) (Exp. I, 4.7.3).

Let R be the set of \(m \in \mathrm{M}\), \(m \neq 0\), such that \(\mathfrak{g}^m(s_0) \neq 0\). Since \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}_0}\) is reductive, we have \(\mathfrak{g}^0(s_0) = \mathfrak{t}(s_0)\) (we write \(\mathfrak{t} = \mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{T}/\mathrm{S})\)), hence \[ \mathfrak{g}(s_0) = \mathfrak{t}(s_0) \oplus \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha(s_0). \] Since the Modules involved are locally free, we may, after shrinking S, suppose the \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) free and \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha. \] Let then \(\mathrm{T}_\alpha\) be the maximal torus of the kernel of \(\alpha\) (recall, cf. Exp. XII 1.12, that a group of multiplicative type possesses a unique maximal torus; this is moreover essentially trivial by descent, the diagonalizable case being obvious). Let \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}_\alpha)\); the group \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) satisfies the hypotheses of 2.3, and its fiber \((\mathrm{Z}_\alpha)_{\overline{s}_0}\) is reductive, of semisimple rank 1, with maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}_0}\) (1.12). By 2.3, there exists therefore an open subset \(\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) of S, containing \(s_0\), such that \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha|\mathrm{U}_\alpha\) has reductive fibers. Put \(\mathrm{U} = \bigcap_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathrm{U}_\alpha\); by 1.12, \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) is reductive, with maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\), for \(s \in \mathrm{U}\). For every \(s \in \mathrm{U}\), the set of roots of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) with respect to \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\) is identified with R, so we have \(\mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}) = \mathrm{rg}(\mathrm{M})\), \(\mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}) = \mathrm{rg}(\mathrm{R})\) (cf. 1.10). We have thus proved (i) and the first assertion of (ii); it remains to prove that the Weyl group \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{U}}}(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{U}})\) is finite over U, i.e. (6.3) вЂњthat it has the same number of points in each geometric fiberвЂќ; for this, it suffices to remark that the geometric fiber of this group at \(s \in \mathrm{U}\) is determined by the situation \(\mathrm{R} \subset \mathrm{M}\), as the constant group associated with the вЂњabstract Weyl group of this root systemвЂќ, and in particular is independent of the point \(s\), cf. BIBLE, 11-07, th. 2 (see also Exp. XXII, nВ° 3).

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.6

Let G be an S-group affine and smooth over S, with connected fibers. The set of points \(s \in \mathrm{G}\) such that \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) is reductive (resp. semisimple) is an open subset U of S, and the functions \[ s \longmapsto \mathrm{rgred}(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}/\overline{s}) \] \[ s \longmapsto \mathrm{rgss}(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}/\overline{s}) \] are locally constant on U.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 2.7

An S-group (= S-group prescheme) G is said to be reductive (resp. semisimple) if it is affine and smooth over S, with geometric fibers which are connected and reductive (resp. semisimple).
The property of being reductive (resp. semisimple) for an S-group G is stable under base change and local for the (fpqc) topology.

Let G be a reductive S-group. For every torus (resp. maximal torus) Q of G, \(\mathrm{Z}(\mathrm{Q}) = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is reductive (resp. is Q). This follows at once from 2.2 and 1.6. Applying 2.5 to \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\), we deduce that Q is contained (locally for the Г©tale topology) in a maximal torus.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 2.9

Using as usual the technique of EGA IV3, В§ 8, the finite presentation hypotheses and theorem 2.5, one sees that if G is a reductive group over S, there exists an open covering \(\{\mathrm{U}_i\}\) of S such that each \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{U}_i}\) comes by base change from a reductive group over a noetherian ring (in fact an algebra of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\)). Similarly, if G possesses a trivial maximal torus T, one may moreover suppose that \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{U}_i}\) comes from a trivial maximal torus of the preceding group, вЂ¦.

pages.title: 3. Roots and root systems of reductive group schemes

blocks.html: 3. Roots and root systems of reductive group schemes

Let S be a prescheme and T an S-torus acting linearly (Exp. I) on a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module of finite type \(\mathscr{F}\). For every character \(\alpha\) of T (i.e., \(\alpha \in \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\)), one defines a subfunctor of \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) by \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})^\alpha(\mathrm{S}') = \{x \in \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})(\mathrm{S}') \mid t\,x = \alpha(t)x \text{ for all } t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}''),\ \mathrm{S}'' \to \mathrm{S}'\}. \] Then \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})^\alpha = \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}^\alpha)\), where \(\mathscr{F}^\alpha\) is a sub-Module of \(\mathscr{F}\), locally a direct factor in \(\mathscr{F}\), hence also locally free.

Indeed, the assertion is local for the (fpqc) topology, and we may suppose \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbf{D}_{\mathrm{S}}(\mathrm{M})\), where M is a finitely generated (free) abelian group. Then \(\alpha\) is identified with a locally constant function from S to M (Exp. VIII 1.3), and after shrinking S we may suppose that this function is constant. We are then reduced to Exp. I, 4.7.3.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.2

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and of finite presentation over S, with connected fibers, and T a torus of G. We say that the character \(\alpha\) of T is a root of G with respect to T if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) For each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\alpha_s\) is a root of \(\mathrm{G}_s\) with respect to \(\mathrm{T}_s\) (1.10).

(ii) \(\alpha\) is nontrivial on each fiber and \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha(s) \neq 0\) for each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\).

(We write \(\mathfrak{g} = \mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{G}/\mathrm{S})\) and let T act on \(\mathfrak{g}\) through the adjoint representation of G).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.3

Let S be a prescheme, T an S-torus, and \(\alpha\) a character of T. The following conditions are equivalent:

(i) \(\alpha\) is nontrivial on each fiber, that is: for every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\alpha_{\overline{s}}\) is distinct from the unit character of \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\).

(ii) For every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm{S}' \neq \varnothing\), \(\alpha_{\mathrm{S}'}\) is distinct from the unit character of \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

(iii) The morphism \(\alpha\) is faithfully flat.

We have (ii) \(\Leftrightarrow\) (i) by Exp. IX 5.2, and (iii) \(\Rightarrow\) (i). To prove (i) \(\Rightarrow\) (iii), we reduce to the case where T is diagonalizable and conclude by Exp. VIII 3.2 a).

Suppose, under the conditions of 3.2, that G is reductive. Then \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) is locally free of rank one (1.12). Moreover, if \(\alpha\) is a root of G with respect to T, then so is \(-\alpha\) (1.10). In particular, if G is of semisimple rank 1, we have by 1.11:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.5

Let S be a prescheme, G a reductive S-group of semisimple rank 1, and T a maximal torus of G. If \(\alpha\) is a root of G with respect to T, then so is \(-\alpha\), and we have \[ \mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}, \] where \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) and \(\mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) are locally free of rank 1.

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 3.6

Let S be a prescheme, G a reductive S-group, T a maximal torus of G, and R a set of roots of G with respect to T. We say that R is a root system of G with respect to T if the following equivalent conditions are satisfied:

(i) For each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(\alpha \mapsto \alpha_{\overline{s}}\) is a bijection from R onto the set of roots of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) with respect to \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\).

(ii) The elements of R are distinct on each fiber (i.e. if \(\alpha, \alpha' \in \mathrm{R}\), \(\alpha \neq \alpha'\), then \(\alpha - \alpha'\) \((= \alpha\,\alpha'^{-1})\) is nontrivial on each fiber), and for each \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) we have \[ \dim(\mathrm{G}_s/k(s)) - \dim(\mathrm{T}_s/k(s)) = \mathrm{Card}(\mathrm{R}). \]

(iii) We have \(\mathfrak{g} = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \coprod_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \mathfrak{g}^\alpha\).

The equivalence of these various conditions is trivial.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 3.7

Let S be a prescheme, G a reductive S-group, T a maximal torus of G, and R a root system of G with respect to T. Every root of G with respect to T is locally on S equal to an element of R.
This is visible from condition (iii).

Put \(\mathscr{M} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\); it is a twisted constant S-group prescheme (Exp. X 5.6). The inclusion \(\mathrm{R} \hookrightarrow \mathscr{M}(\mathrm{S})\) defines a morphism \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}} \to \mathscr{M}\), where \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}}\) is the constant S-prescheme defined by R; thanks to condition (ii), one sees easily that this morphism is an open and closed immersion whose image is none other than \(\bigcup_{\alpha \in \mathrm{R}} \alpha(\mathrm{S})\) (each \(\alpha \in \mathrm{R}\) being considered as a section \(\mathrm{S} \to \mathscr{M}\)).

Let \(\mathscr{R}\) be the functor of roots of G with respect to T: by definition, \(\mathscr{R}(\mathrm{S}')\) is the set of roots of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) with respect to \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\); if \(\mathrm{S}' = \varnothing\), then \(\mathscr{M}(\mathrm{S}') = \mathscr{R}(\mathrm{S}') = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{loc.const.}}(\mathrm{S}', \mathrm{R}) = e\); otherwise \(\mathrm{R} \to \mathscr{M}(\mathrm{S}')\) identifies R with a root system of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\) with respect to \(\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{S}'}\), and we have \(\mathscr{R}(\mathrm{S}') = \mathrm{Hom}_{\mathrm{loc.const.}}(\mathrm{S}', \mathrm{R})\), by 3.7; this shows that \(\mathscr{R}\) is representable by \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}}\). If now we no longer necessarily suppose that G possesses a root system relative to T, \(\mathscr{R}\) is in any case a subsheaf of \(\mathscr{M}\) for (fpqc). Locally for this topology, G possesses a root system with respect to T (take for example T trivial). By Exp. IV 4.6.8 and the theory of descent of open (resp. closed) subpreschemes, we obtain

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 3.8

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group, and T a maximal torus of G. The functor \(\mathscr{R}\) of roots of G with respect to T is representable by a finite twisted constant S-prescheme (Exp. X 5.1) which is an open and closed subprescheme of \(\underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\). For \(\mathrm{R} \subset \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\) to be a root system of G with respect to T, it is necessary and sufficient that the morphism \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}} \to \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{T}, \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}})\) defined by the preceding inclusion induce an isomorphism \(\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{S}} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathscr{R}\).

Let S be a prescheme, G a reductive S-group, T a maximal torus of G, and \(\alpha\) a root of G with respect to T (i.e. a section of \(\mathscr{R}\)). Consider the kernel \(\mathrm{Ker}(\alpha)\) of \(\mathscr{R}\), its unique maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_\alpha\), and the centralizer of the latter, \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}_\alpha)\). It is an S-group closed in G, reductive (2.8) of semisimple rank 1 (1.12). Moreover \(\underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha/\mathrm{S}) = \mathfrak{t} \oplus \mathfrak{g}^\alpha \oplus \mathfrak{g}^{-\alpha}\) (1.12), hence \(\{\alpha, -\alpha\}\) is a root system of \(\mathrm{Z}_\alpha\) with respect to T.

Let S be a prescheme, G a reductive S-group, T a maximal torus of G, and \(\alpha\) a root of G with respect to T. If \(k\alpha\), \(k \in \mathbb{Q}\), is a root of G with respect to T, then \(k = 1\) or \(k = -1\). This follows at once from 1.12.
4. Roots and vector group schemes

Let S be a prescheme and \(\mathscr{F}\) a locally free \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module of finite type. The S-prescheme \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})\) is smooth over S. Its Lie Algebra is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathscr{F}\). Indeed, we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})/\mathrm{S}) = \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})/\mathrm{S})). \] (Exp. II cor. to def. 4.4 and the Examples preceding 4.5). We shall always identify \(\mathscr{F}\) and \(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{F})/\mathrm{S})\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.2

Let S be a prescheme and V a vector bundle over S, smooth over S. There then exists a unique isomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Modules \[ \exp : \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{V}/\mathrm{S})) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{V} \] which induces the identity on the Lie Algebras.

Indeed, we have \(\mathrm{V} = \mathbf{V}(\mathrm{F})\) for a certain quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module H. Then we have a canonical isomorphism \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{V}/\mathrm{S})) = \underline{\mathrm{Lie}}(\mathrm{V}/\mathrm{S}) \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathrm{V} \] (Exp. II loc. cit.). Then \(\mathrm{H} \simeq \mathscr{L}ie(\mathrm{V}/\mathrm{S})^\vee\) is locally free of finite type, hence \(\mathrm{V} \simeq \mathbf{W}(\mathrm{H}^\vee)\), and the uniqueness of \(\exp\) follows at once, since \(\mathbf{W}\) is fully faithful.

If V is a vector bundle over S, we shall denote by \(\mathrm{V}^*\) the open subset of V obtained by removing from it the zero section. Let us write the group law of V in multiplicative notation. The action of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\) on V defining the module structure will then be written exponentially \[ \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}} \underset{\mathrm{S}}{\times} \mathrm{V} \longrightarrow \mathrm{V}, \qquad (x, v) \longmapsto v^x. \] We have \((vv')^x = v^x v'^x\), \(v^{x+x'} = v^x v^{x'}\), \(v^{xx'} = (v^x)^{x'}\). In particular, if we restrict the operator law to \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\), \(\mathrm{V}^*\) is stable and is thus endowed with the structure of an object with operator group \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\). We shall again write this law as \((z, v) \mapsto v^z\).

In particular, let \(\mathscr{L}\) be an invertible Module on S and \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})\) the associated \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module. Then \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})^*\) is a principal homogeneous bundle under \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) (locally trivial), which is none other than the principal bundle associated with the vector bundle \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})\). We write \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathscr{L})^* = \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})^*(\mathrm{S})\). There is a bijective correspondence between the isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Modules \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}} \simeq \mathscr{L}\), the isomorphisms of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-modules \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}} \simeq \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})\) and the sections \(\mathrm{S} \to \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})^*\). This correspondence is given by \(f \mapsto \mathbf{W}(f) \mapsto \mathbf{W}(f)(1)\). It is compatible with base extension. We may therefore regard \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})^*\) as the вЂњscheme of trivializations of \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})\)вЂќ.

Let S be a prescheme, T a torus over S, P an S-group with operator group \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) (for example a vector bundle), and \(\alpha\) a character of T. We denote by \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathrm{P}\) the semidirect product of P by T, with T acting on it through the composite morphism \[ \mathrm{T} \xrightarrow{\ \alpha\ } \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \longrightarrow \underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{P}). \]

blocks.title: Definition
blocks.html:
Definition 4.6

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme, T a subgroup of G, \(\alpha\) a character of T, and \(\mathscr{L}\) an \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module. Let \[ p : \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] be a morphism of groups. We say that \(p\) is normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\) if it satisfies the following equivalent conditions:

(i) \(p\) is a morphism of objects with operator group T, if we let T act on \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})\) through \(\alpha\) and on G by inner automorphisms. In other words, for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and all \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(x \in \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})(\mathrm{S}') = \Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathscr{L} \otimes \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}})\), we have \[ \mathrm{int}(t)p(x) = p(\alpha(t)x). \]

(ii) The morphism \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}) \to \mathrm{G}\) defined by the product in G (i.e. by \((t, x) \mapsto t \cdot p(x)\)) is a morphism of groups.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.7

Under the conditions of 4.6, suppose moreover that G is smooth, of finite presentation, and with connected fibers, that T is a maximal torus of G, and that \(\mathscr{L}\) is invertible. If \(p\) is a monomorphism and \(\alpha\) is nonzero on each fiber, then \(\alpha\) is a root of G with respect to T.
Indeed, \(\mathscr{L}ie(p) : \mathscr{L} \to \mathfrak{g}\) is a monomorphism which factors through \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.8

Under the conditions of 4.7, suppose that G is reductive and that \(p\) is a monomorphism. Then \(\alpha\) is a root of G with respect to T and \(\mathscr{L}ie(p)\) induces an isomorphism: \[ \mathscr{L}ie(p) : \mathscr{L} \xrightarrow{\ \sim\ } \mathfrak{g}^\alpha. \]

Indeed, by virtue of 4.7 and the fact that \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha\) is invertible, it suffices to prove that \(\alpha\) is nonzero on each fiber. So let \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) be such that \(\alpha_{\overline{s}} = 0\) (= 1 in multiplicative notation). If X is a nonzero section of \(\mathscr{L} \otimes_{\mathrm{S}} k(\overline{s})\), then \(p(\mathrm{X})\) is a unipotent element \(\neq e\) of \(\mathrm{G}(\overline{s})\) which centralizes \(\mathrm{T}_{\overline{s}}\), which is impossible, since the latter is its own centralizer.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.9

Under the conditions of 4.8, there exists a monomorphism of groups with operators T (i.e. normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\)) \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha) \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] which induces on the Lie Algebras the canonical morphism \(\mathfrak{g}^\alpha \to \mathfrak{g}\).
We shall soon see that 4.9 in fact holds whenever G is a reductive group and \(\alpha\) a root of G with respect to T, and that such a morphism is unique.

blocks.title: Reminder
blocks.html:
Reminder 4.10

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field, G a reductive \(k\)-group, T a maximal torus of G, and \(\alpha\) a root of G with respect to T. There exists a monomorphism \[ p : \mathbb{G}_{a,k} \longrightarrow \mathrm{G} \] normalized by T with multiplier \(\alpha\).
See BIBLE, 13-05, th. 1.

Let us end this section with a technical result which will be useful to us. Let S be a prescheme and \(\mathscr{L}\) an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module. Let \(q\) be an integer \(> 0\) such that \(x \mapsto x^q\) is an endomorphism of the S-group \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\). (If \(\mathrm{S} \neq \varnothing\), we have \(q = 1\), or \(q = p^n\), \(p\) being a prime number zero on S; this follows at once from the following elementary fact: the g.c.d. of the binomial coefficients \(\binom{q}{i}\), for \(i \neq 0, q\), is \(p\) if \(q = p^n\), \(p\) prime, and 1 otherwise). The morphism defined by the \(q\)-th power \[ \mathscr{L} \longrightarrow \mathscr{L}^{\otimes q} \] is a morphism of sheaves of abelian groups. It defines by base change a morphism of S-group preschemes: \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}) \longrightarrow \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}^{\otimes q}). \] In particular, if \(\mathscr{L}'\) is another invertible Module and if we have a morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Modules \[ h : \mathscr{L}^{\otimes q} \longrightarrow \mathscr{L}', \] we deduce from it a morphism of S-group preschemes: \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}) \longrightarrow \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}') \] by \(x \mapsto h(x^q)\).
With these notations, we have

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.12

Let S be a prescheme, T (resp. \(\mathrm{T}'\)) an S-torus, \(\mathscr{L}\) (resp. \(\mathscr{L}'\)) an invertible \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module, and \(\alpha\) (resp. \(\alpha'\)) a character of T (resp. \(\mathrm{T}'\)); consider the semidirect product \(\mathrm{T} \cdot_\alpha \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})\), resp. \(\mathrm{T}' \cdot_{\alpha'} \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}')\). Let \[ f : \mathrm{T} \longrightarrow \mathrm{T}' \] be a morphism of groups and \[ g : \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}) \longrightarrow \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}') \] a morphism (not necessarily multiplicative) satisfying the following condition: \[ g(\alpha(t)x) = \alpha'(f(t))\,g(x) \] for all \(x \in \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})(\mathrm{S}')\), \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\).

Let \(s_0 \in \mathrm{S}\) be such that \(\alpha_{\overline{s}_0} \neq e\).

a) Suppose that \(g\) sends the zero section to the zero section and that for every integer \(n > 0\) we have \((\alpha' \circ f)_{\overline{s}_0} \neq n\,\alpha_{\overline{s}_0}\). Then \(g = 0\) in a neighborhood of \(s_0\).

b) Suppose that \(g\) is a morphism of groups such that \(g_{\overline{s}_0} \neq 0\). There then exist an open subset U of S containing \(s_0\) and an integer \(q > 0\) such that \(x \mapsto x^q\) is an endomorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{U}}\) and \((\alpha' \circ f)_{\mathrm{U}} = q\,\alpha_{\mathrm{U}}\).

c) Suppose that \((\alpha' \circ f)_{\overline{s}_0} = q\,\alpha_{\overline{s}_0}\), where \(q\) is an integer \(> 0\) such that \(x \mapsto x^q\) is an endomorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\). There then exist an open subset U of S containing \(s_0\) and a unique morphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Modules \[ h : \mathscr{L}^{\otimes q}|\mathrm{U} \longrightarrow \mathscr{L}'|\mathrm{U} \] such that \(g_{\mathrm{U}}\) is the composite morphism \[ \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L})_{\mathrm{U}} \xrightarrow{x \mapsto x^q} \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}^{\otimes q})_{\mathrm{U}} \xrightarrow{\mathbf{W}(h)} (\mathscr{L}')_{\mathrm{U}}. \]

Since the conclusion is local on S, we may suppose that \(\mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}) = \mathbf{W}(\mathscr{L}') = \mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) and hence that \(g\) is expressed as a polynomial \[ g(\mathrm{X}) = \sum_{n \geqslant 0} a_n \mathrm{X}^n, \qquad a_n \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}). \] The condition linking \(f\) and \(g\) can be written as an identity in \(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'})[\mathrm{X}]\): \[ \sum_{n \geqslant 0} a_n \alpha'(f(t))\mathrm{X}^n = \sum_{n \geqslant 0} a_n \alpha(t)^n \mathrm{X}^n, \] that is, for every \(n \geqslant 0\), every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and every \(t \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}')\), \[ a_n(\alpha'(f(t)) - \alpha(t)^n) = 0. \] For each \(n \geqslant 0\), let \(\mathrm{S}_n\) be the set of \(s \in \mathrm{S}\) such that \((\alpha' \circ f)_{\overline{s}} = n\,\alpha_{\overline{s}}\). We know (Exp. IX 5.3) that the \(\mathrm{S}_n\) are open and closed, and that \((\alpha' \circ f)_{\mathrm{S}_n} = n\,\alpha_{\mathrm{S}_n}\). Moreover, since \(\alpha_{\overline{s}_0} \neq e\), we may, after shrinking S, suppose that \(\alpha\) is nonzero on each fiber (same reference), which implies that the \(\mathrm{S}_n\) are disjoint. After shrinking S, we may therefore suppose that we are in one of the following two cases: there exists an \(n\) with \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{S}_n\), or else all the \(\mathrm{S}_n\) are empty.
Let \(m \geqslant 0\) be such that \(\mathrm{S}_m = \varnothing\); I claim that then \(a_m = 0\); indeed \(\alpha' \circ f\) and \(m\,\alpha\) are distinct on each fiber of S, and we have:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.13

Let S be a prescheme, T an S-torus, and \(\alpha\) and \(\alpha'\) two characters of S distinct on each fiber; there exist a covering family for (fpqc): \(\{\mathrm{S}_i \to \mathrm{S}\}\), and for each \(i\) a \(t_i \in \mathrm{T}(\mathrm{S}_i)\), such that \(\alpha(t_i) - \alpha'(t_i) = 1\).
The lemma is trivial, by reduction to the diagonalizable case, then to the case \(\mathrm{T} = \mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\).

Let us return to the proof; we have just proved (a). In cases (b) and (c), there exists an \(n\) with \(\mathrm{S} = \mathrm{S}_n\) \((n = q\) in (c)). By the preceding result we therefore have \(a_m = 0\) for \(m \neq n\), which proves that \(g\) can be written \[ g(\mathrm{X}) = a_n \mathrm{X}^n, \qquad a_n \in \Gamma(\mathrm{S}, \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}). \] This proves (c) at once. In case (b), we know that \(a_n(s_0) \neq 0\), so we may suppose \(a_n\) invertible on S, which implies that \(x \mapsto x^n\) is an endomorphism of \(\mathbb{G}_{a,\mathrm{S}}\) (by virtue of the hypothesis of (b)) and finishes the proof.
5. An instructive example

Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field of characteristic 0. Put \(\mathrm{A} = k[t]\), the ring of polynomials in one variable over \(k\), and \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\). Consider the following Lie Algebra \(\mathfrak{g}\) over \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\): as a Module, it is free of dimension 3, with basis \(\{\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}, \mathrm{H}\}\); the multiplication table is \[ [\mathrm{X}, \mathrm{Y}] = 2t\mathrm{H}, \quad [\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{X}] = \mathrm{X}, \quad [\mathrm{H}, \mathrm{Y}] = -\mathrm{Y}. \] For \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(s \neq s_0\) (the point defined by \(t = 0\)), the reduced fiber \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) is isomorphic to the Lie algebra of the group \(\mathrm{PGL}_{2,k(s)}\). For \(s = s_0\), it is a solvable Lie algebra.

Let \(\mathrm{G}_1\) be the group scheme of automorphisms of \(\mathfrak{g}\): for every \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\), \(\mathrm{G}_1(\mathrm{S}')\) is the group of automorphisms of the \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\)-Lie Algebra \(\mathfrak{g} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\). It is a closed subprescheme of the group \(\mathrm{GL}(\mathfrak{g})\) of automorphisms of the \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module \(\mathfrak{g}\). Let \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) and \(u \in \mathrm{M}_3(\Gamma(\mathrm{S}', \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}))\), regarded as an endomorphism of the \(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}\)-Module \(\mathfrak{g} \otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}} \mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}'}\): \[ \begin{aligned} u(\mathrm{X}) &= a\mathrm{X} + b\mathrm{Y} + e\mathrm{H}, \\ u(\mathrm{Y}) &= b'\mathrm{X} + a'\mathrm{Y} + e'\mathrm{H}, \\ u(\mathrm{H}) &= c\mathrm{X} + c'\mathrm{Y} + d\mathrm{H}. \end{aligned} \] One sees at once that \(u\) is a section of \(\mathrm{G}_1\) if and only if \(\mathrm{dГ©t}(u)\) is invertible and the following relations hold: \[ \begin{aligned} &(1)\ \ a(d-1) = ec, & &(1')\ \ a'(d-1) = e'c', \\ &(2)\ \ b(d+1) = ec', & &(2')\ \ b'(d+1) = e'c, \\ &(3)\ \ e = 2t(bc - ac'), & &(3')\ \ e' = 2t(b'c' - a'c), \\ &(4)\ \ 2tc = eb' - ae', & &(4')\ \ 2tc' = be' - ea', \\ &(5)\ \ 2t(aa' - bb') = 2td. & & \end{aligned} \]

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.3

Relations (1) вЂ¦ (5) imply \[ \mathrm{dГ©t}(u) = aa'(2 - d) + bb'(d + 2), \] \[ aa' - bb' = d \cdot \mathrm{dГ©t}(u). \]

Indeed, the first assertion is obtained at once by substituting relations (1), (1'), (2), (2') into the expansion of \(\mathrm{dГ©t}(u)\): \[ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{dГ©t}(u) &= aa'd + be'c + b'c'e - a'ec - ae'c' - bb'd \\ &= aa'd + bb'(d+1) + bb'(d+1) - aa'(d-1) - bb'd - aa'(d-1) \\ &= aa'(d - d + 1 - d + 1) + bb'(d + 1 + d + 1 - d) \\ &= aa'(2 - d) + bb'(d + 2). \end{aligned} \] Multiplying this relation by \(d\), we obtain \[ d \cdot \mathrm{dГ©t}(u) = aa'(2d - d^2) + bb'(d^2 + 2d). \] But the relation \((1) \times (1') = (2) \times (2')\) gives at once \[ aa'(d-1)^2 = bb'(d+1)^2. \] Combining the two preceding relations, we immediately find the second desired formula.

Now consider \(\mathrm{G} = \mathrm{G}_1 \cap \mathrm{SL}(\mathfrak{g})\). It is the closed subgroup of \(\mathrm{G}_1\) defined by the equation \(\mathrm{dГ©t}(u) = 1\). It is therefore an affine group over S.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.5

The group G is smooth over S.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.6

Let U be the open subset of \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathfrak{g}) \simeq \mathbf{W}(\mathrm{M}_3(\mathscr{O}_{\mathrm{S}}))\) defined by the condition вЂњ\(ad\) invertibleвЂќ, i.e. the open subset \(\underline{\mathrm{End}}_{\mathrm{A}}(\mathfrak{g})_f\), where \(f\) is the function defined by \(f(u) = ad\). Then \(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{G}\) is the closed subprescheme of U defined by the six equations: \[ (1), \quad (2), \quad (2'), \quad (3), \quad (3') \quad \text{and} \quad (\mathrm{D}) : aa' - bb' = d. \]

It is first of all clear that these six relations are satisfied by every вЂњpointвЂќ of G (lemma 5.3), in particular by every вЂњpointвЂќ of \(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{G}\). Conversely, we must show that if \(u \in \mathrm{U}(\mathrm{S}')\) (for any \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\)), and if \(u\) satisfies the six conditions of the statement, then \(\mathrm{dГ©t}(u) = 1\) and \(u\) also satisfies (1'), (4), (4') and (5).

First we have \((\mathrm{D}) \Rightarrow (5)\). Combining (2) and (2'), we get at once \[ bb'(d+1) = bce' = b'c'e. \] But by (3) and (3'), writing \(2t(bc - ac')(b'c' - a'c)\) in two ways, we have: \[ (bc - ac')e' = (b'c' - a'c)e. \] Combining with the preceding relation, this gives \(ac'e' = a'ce\). But by (1), \(a'ce = a'a(d-1)\), which proves \(a(a'(d-1) - e'c') = 0\) and implies (1'), \(a\) being supposed invertible.

Let us prove (4) and (4'). Let us do it for (4'), for example, the other computation following by symmetry. By (3) and (3'), we have at once \[ a'e + be' = -2t(aa' - bb')c' = -2tdc'. \] Combining (1') and (2), we have at once \[ a'e + be' = d(be' - ea'), \] which finishes the proof of (4'), \(d\) being supposed invertible. By 5.3, (D) implies \(\mathrm{dГ©t}(u) = 1\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.7

G is smooth over S along the unit section.

By 5.6 and SGA 1, II 4.10, it suffices to prove that the differentials of the functions \[ a(d-1) - ec, \quad b(d+1) - ec', \quad b'(d+1) - e'c, \] \[ e - 2t(bc - ac'), \quad e' - 2t(b'c' - a'c), \quad aa' - bb' - d, \] at the points of the unit section of G are linearly independent. Now, denoting by a capital letter the differential of the corresponding lowercase letter, these are \[ \mathrm{D}, \quad 2\mathrm{B}, \quad 2\mathrm{B}', \quad \mathrm{E} + 2t\mathrm{C}', \quad \mathrm{E}' + 2t\mathrm{C}, \quad \mathrm{A} + \mathrm{A}' + \mathrm{B}, \] which are indeed linearly independent modulo every \((t - a)\), \(a \in \mathrm{A}\).

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.8

For \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), \(s \neq s_0\), the fiber \(\mathrm{G}_s\) is connected and semisimple.

Indeed, \(\mathfrak{g}(s)\) is isomorphic to the Lie Algebra of \(\mathrm{PL}_{2\,k(s)}\); we have \(\mathrm{G}_s = \mathrm{G}_{1\,s}\) smooth over \(k(s)\), and it is well known that the automorphism group of the Lie algebra of \(\mathrm{PL}_2\) over a field of characteristic 0 is \(\mathrm{PL}_2\) itself.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.9

The fiber \(\mathrm{G}_{s_0}\) is solvable and has two connected components, which are of the following form: \[ \mathrm{G}^0_{s_0} = \left\{ \begin{pmatrix} a & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & a^{-1} & 0 \\ c & c' & 1 \end{pmatrix} \right\} \quad \text{and} \quad \mathrm{G}^-_{s_0} = \left\{ \begin{pmatrix} 0 & b & 0 \\ b^{-1} & 0 & 0 \\ c & c' & -1 \end{pmatrix} \right\}. \]
Indeed, we have \(e = e' = 0\), since \(t = 0\) at \(s_0\). One then solves equations (1) to (5) and (D) immediately.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 5.10

\(w = \begin{pmatrix} 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}\) is a section of G over S, such that \(w(s_0) \in \mathrm{G}^-_{s_0}\).

Let us now prove 5.5. We know that if we denote by \(\mathrm{G}^0\) the union of the identity components of the fibers of G (that is, the complement of \(\mathrm{G}^-_{s_0}\)), then \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is an open subgroup of G smooth over S, by 5.7. Since, by translation, G is obviously smooth at the points of \(w(\mathrm{S})\), G is smooth over S.

Consider the morphism \(\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}} \to \mathrm{G}^0\) defined by \(z \mapsto \begin{pmatrix} z & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1/z & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}\). It is a monomorphism which defines a torus T of \(\mathrm{G}^0\). I claim that \[ \mathrm{T} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_0}(\mathrm{T}). \] It suffices indeed to verify the first equality. Since these are subgroups of G smooth over S, it suffices to verify that they have the same geometric points. For the fibers at points \(s \neq s_0\), this follows from the fact that \(\mathrm{PGL}_{2,k(s)}\) is reductive and that \(\mathrm{T}_s\) is a maximal torus of it for dimension reasons (cf. 1.11). On the fiber at \(s_0\), the computation is immediate. In particular, it follows that T is a maximal torus of G and of \(\mathrm{G}^0\).

The section \(w\) of G defined in 5.9 normalizes T. It follows at once (cf. 2.4) that the Weyl group of G is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}\), and in particular is finite. \[ \mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})/\mathrm{T} = (\mathbb{Z}/2\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}. \] On the other hand, \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}^0}(\mathrm{T})\) is not finite: it is вЂњmissing a pointвЂќ above \(s_0\).

The open immersion \(\mathrm{G}^0 \to \mathrm{G}\) is not a closed immersion (for \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is dense in G); it is however an affine morphism (and hence \(\mathrm{G}^0\) is affine over S). Indeed, since \(\mathrm{G}^0_{s_0}\) is closed in \(\mathrm{G}_{s_0}\), which is closed in G, the complement U of \(\mathrm{G}^0_{s_0}\) in G is open; \(\mathrm{G}^0\) and U form an open covering of G, and it suffices to verify that the immersions \(\mathrm{G}^0 \to \mathrm{G}^0\) and \(\mathrm{G}^0 \cap \mathrm{U} \to \mathrm{U}\) are affine; for the first this is trivial, and for the second one remarks that \(\mathrm{U} \cap \mathrm{G}^0\) is defined in U by the equation: \(t \neq 0\).

We have thus constructed an affine smooth S-group with connected fibers, \(\mathrm{G}^0\), possessing a maximal torus T which is its own centralizer and whose Weyl group \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}^0}(\mathrm{T})\) is not finite.

pages.title: 6. Local existence of maximal tori. The Weyl group

blocks.html: 6. Local existence of maximal tori. The Weyl group

In the course of the proof of 2.5, we used a result of Exp. XI on the local existence, for the Г©tale topology, of maximal tori; the proof of Exp. XI uses a delicate representability result (XI 4.1). In the particular case at hand, one can give another proof of it, based on the ideas of Exp. XII nВ° 7, and of a much more elementary nature.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.1

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth, affine and with connected fibers over S, and \(s_0\) a point of S such that the maximal tori of the geometric fiber \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}_0}\) are their own centralizers. There exist an Г©tale morphism \(\mathrm{S}' \to \mathrm{S}\) covering \(\mathrm{S}_0\), and a trivial maximal torus T of \(\mathrm{G}_{\mathrm{S}'}\).

First, we may suppose S affine. Since G is of finite presentation over S, we may suppose S noetherian, then local, then henselian with separably closed residue field (cf. EGA IV, 8.12, В§ 8.8, and В§ 18.8). So put \(\mathrm{S} = \operatorname{Spec}(\mathrm{A})\), with A henselian with separably closed residue field \(k = k(s_0)\). Choose a maximal torus \(\mathrm{T}_0\) of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) \((= \mathrm{G}_k)\) (one exists, for example because the scheme of maximal tori of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is smooth over \(k\), Exp. XII, 7.1 c)); since \(k\) is separably closed, \(\mathrm{T}_0\) is trivial and is therefore given by a monomorphism of groups \[ f_0 : \mathbb{G}_{m,k}^r \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_0. \] Let \(m\) be an integer \(> 1\) prime to the characteristic of \(k\). For every \(h > 0\), \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_0}({}_{m^h}\mathrm{T}_0)\) is representable by a closed subprescheme of \(\mathrm{G}_0\) (Exp. VIII 6.7). Since the \({}_{m^h}\mathrm{T}_0\) are schematically dense in \(\mathrm{T}_0\) (cf. Exp. IX 4.10) and \(\mathrm{G}_0\) is noetherian, there exists an \(h\) such that \[ \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_0}({}_{m^h}\mathrm{T}_0) = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}_0}(\mathrm{T}_0) = \mathrm{T}_0. \] Put \(n = m^h\); since \(n\) is invertible on S, \({}_n\mathbb{G}_{m,\mathrm{S}}\) is isomorphic to \((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}\); \(f_0\) therefore defines a monomorphism of groups \[ g_0 : (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_k^r \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}_0 \] such that \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(g_0) = \mathrm{T}_0\). Now the S-functor \[ \mathrm{P} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}^r, \mathrm{G}) \] is representable by an S-prescheme of finite type (as a closed subprescheme of \({}_n\mathrm{G} = \underline{\mathrm{Hom}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}((\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}, \mathrm{G}) = \mathrm{Ker}(\mathrm{G} \xrightarrow{n} \mathrm{G})\)). But P is smooth over S (Exp. IX 3.6), so \(g_0 \in \mathrm{P}(k)\) lifts to a section \(g \in \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{S})\) (Hensel's lemma, Exp. XI 1.11): \[ g : (\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z})_{\mathrm{S}}^r \longrightarrow \mathrm{G}. \] Consider \(\mathrm{H} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(g)\); it is a closed subgroup prescheme of G, smooth over S (Exp. IX, 3.3), and \(\mathrm{H}_0 = \mathrm{T}_0\) by hypothesis. Let then \(\mathrm{H}^0\) be the identity component of H; it is a subgroup prescheme of G, smooth and with connected fibers, whose special fiber is a torus. By Exp. X 8.1, it is a torus, necessarily trivial (Exp. X 4.6). Put \(\mathrm{H}^0 = \mathrm{T}\) and let \(\mathrm{C} = \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{T})\), which is a closed subgroup of G (Exp. VIII 6.5 e), smooth (Exp. XI 2.4). Consider \(\mathrm{C}^0\) (we have in fact \(\mathrm{C}^0 = \mathrm{C}\), but we do not need to know this); \(\mathrm{C}^0 \supset \mathrm{T}\) are two smooth groups with connected fibers. They coincide at \(s_0\), hence in a neighborhood. After shrinking S, we may therefore suppose \(\mathrm{C}^0 = \mathrm{T}\), hence a fortiori T maximal.

blocks.title: Remark
blocks.html:
Remark 6.2

The proof shows in particular that the reductive rank of \(\mathrm{G}_{\overline{s}}\) is constant in a neighborhood of \(s = s_0\).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 6.3

Let S be a prescheme, G an S-group prescheme smooth and of finite presentation over S, and Q a subtorus of G.

(i) \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) are representable by closed subgroup preschemes, smooth (and hence of finite presentation) over S.

(ii) \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is an open and closed subprescheme of \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\). The quotient \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q}) = \underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})/\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is representable by an open subgroup prescheme of \(\underline{\mathrm{Aut}}_{\mathrm{S}\text{-gr.}}(\mathrm{Q})\); it is therefore an S-group prescheme quasi-finite, Г©tale and separated over S.

(iii) For every \(s \in \mathrm{S}\), put \[ w(s) = (\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}(\overline{s})}(\mathrm{Q}(\overline{s})) : \underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}(\overline{s})}(\mathrm{Q}(\overline{s}))). \] Then \(s \mapsto w(s)\) is lower semicontinuous, and it is constant in a neighborhood of \(s\) if and only if \(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) is finite over S in a neighborhood of \(s\).

By Exp. XI 6.11, \(\underline{\mathrm{Centr}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) and \(\underline{\mathrm{Norm}}_{\mathrm{G}}(\mathrm{Q})\) are representable by closed subpreschemes of finite presentation of G. These are smooth by Exp. XI 2.4 and 2.4 bis, which proves (i). Assertions (ii) and (iii) are then proved as in Exp. XI 5.9 and 5.10, whose proof in fact uses only (i) and not the delicate theorems Exp. XI 4.1 and 4.2.
